lens.included_activities: Independent commercial-equipment distributors in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band that repeatedly serve external restaurant, food-retail, institutional, or other commercial customers with equipment specification, project coordination, installation, preventive maintenance, repair, parts, or related accountable service.
lens.excluded_activities: Product-only catalog sellers, manufacturers and captive sales branches, consumer retailers, pure rental companies, commission-only agents, internal maintenance units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers purchase commercial machines, fixtures, shelving, scales, and related equipment at product margins, then buy repeat project, installation, maintenance, repair, parts, or annual-service work through contracts and work orders.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code is heterogeneous across restaurant equipment, store fixtures, shelving, scales, and other commercial machines, and many establishments are product-only. The lens is narrowed to distributors with a material recurring or repeat accountable service component to keep the screen coherent.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for the broader 423400 professional and commercial equipment wholesale industry, while Global Industrial reports its own distributor workforce; neither measures wage-weighted not-yet-automated tasks in eligible 423440 firms. I map the observed sales, customer service, administration, IT, product management, and support work to general task-level AI evidence, while treating warehouse, installation, and field-repair work as much less exposed. The public distributor and broad NAICS mix may overstate digital scale and understate field labor in LMM service dealers.
a.rationale: Quoting, product selection, proposal drafting, order entry, scheduling, parts identification, service triage, documentation, purchasing, and back-office work are materially exposed. BLS identifies technical sales, other sales, customer service, management, software, and support among the largest 423400 occupations; Global Industrial reports 36% of staff customer-facing and 37% in administrative functions, but physical distribution and equipment service remain important.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted O*NET task inventory and current-automation audit from a representative sample of 423440 equipment distributors, split among inside sales, project teams, warehouse, installers, and field technicians.
caveats: Occupation evidence is at 423400, not six-digit 423440.
Global Industrial spans broader industrial products and is much larger than the target firms.
General AI evidence measures exposure or use, not removable labor.

rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should be feasible for product search, quote preparation, service intake, dispatch support, technician knowledge retrieval, invoice matching, and routine customer communications. Realization is limited by fragmented catalogs and legacy ERPs, sparse equipment histories, safety and warranty rules, technician acceptance, and the need for human verification before specifying or servicing physical equipment.
rho.change_evidence: Measured deployments at multi-branch equipment distributors showing adoption, exception and rework rates, technician utilization, quote cycle time, hours removed, and sustained hiring avoidance by workflow.
caveats: No representative 423440 implementation study was found.
AI use observed on consumer chat products may not transfer to field-service systems.
Small distributors may lack clean product and service data.

e.rationale: Official NAICS scope is merchant wholesale of commercial machines generally used in restaurants and stores, not a service industry. However, commercial food-equipment evidence shows installation, repair, parts, and separately sold annual service contracts are natural adjuncts to equipment. A meaningful but minority share of the frozen LMM population is therefore likely to meet the repeat-service lens.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level 423440 census classifying LMM distributors by service technicians, maintenance offerings, installation capability, service-contract revenue, and product-only status.
caveats: The frozen n is derived using broad distributor margins that may misclassify service-rich firms by EBITDA band.
Manufacturer service models demonstrate the activity but not its prevalence among independent wholesalers.
Eligibility likely varies sharply by equipment niche.

s5.bridge: Census owner-age evidence covers U.S. employer businesses and demographic groups in wholesale trade rather than observing control transfers in eligible 423440 firms. I convert the large older-owner stock into a five-year event probability after discounting for owners who retain, close, transfer internally, or lack transferable operations. Age can overstate near-term sales, while strategic consolidation may create transfers unrelated to retirement.
s5.rationale: Census reports 51% of responding employer-business owners were 55 or older and separately identifies wholesale trade as an older-owner sector. Because owner age is only a precursor, not a transfer, the qualifying five-year probability is set far below the older-owner share.
s5.change_evidence: Closed transaction and closure records from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, and equipment-dealer brokers, matched to a denominator of eligible 423440 LMM firms and tagged for control, succession, and continued operation.
caveats: Broad and subgroup-specific age data are weak predictors of transaction timing.
No denominator-complete six-digit deal series was found.
Family succession and manufacturer dealer-authorizations may affect transferability.

q.rationale: Urgent repair, preventive maintenance, and bundled project work permit more retention than transparent product resale, particularly under annual service contracts. Product channels remain fragmented and price-sensitive: Global Industrial says buyers emphasize price, selection, availability, service, terms, and convenience and face manufacturers, internet sellers, and large retailers. Savings will partly pass through at renewal or competitive bid, but field-service capacity and response accountability provide some insulation.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level gross-profit cohorts for LMM equipment distributors before and after automation, separated into equipment, projects, annual service, break/fix labor, and parts, with renewal repricing and win/loss data.
caveats: Public manufacturer and distributor economics are only directional for independent LMM dealers.
Gross margin is not direct evidence of AI-benefit retention.
Emergency repair and bid equipment sales have very different pricing power.

d5.rationale: Installed commercial equipment creates recurring parts, compliance, repair, and replacement needs, while restaurant openings, remodels, and automation can add demand. Near-term equipment demand is cyclical: Middleby reported a 1.7% organic decline in commercial foodservice equipment sales in 2025 and cited weaker chain traffic and replacement demand. The five-year constant-price range therefore centers near flat with room for recessionary contraction or recovery-led growth.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-price forecast for 423440 shipments and service work by equipment niche, supported by distributor backlog, restaurant and retail openings, installed-base age, replacement cycles, and service-call volumes.
caveats: Middleby is a global manufacturer, not a U.S. merchant wholesaler.
Reported sales include price, currency, acquisition, and share effects.
The code combines end markets with different cycles.

o.rationale: Simple replenishment and product discovery can migrate to marketplaces or manufacturer-direct channels, but eligible firms coordinate physical specification, delivery, installation, acceptance, maintenance, repair, parts, and on-site safety. ITW reports annual service contracts recognized over the service period and on-demand repairs recognized after completion and customer acceptance, illustrating durable accountable-operator work that software alone cannot supply.
o.change_evidence: Customer and service-ticket cohorts measuring current quantity shifted to self-service, manufacturer-direct, remote-only, or software-only delivery, and the share still requiring on-site licensed or accountable provider work.
caveats: The service lens selects activities with intrinsically high operator requirement.
Remote diagnostics and connected equipment could reduce truck rolls.
Manufacturers may internalize service and displace independent distributors without eliminating operator work.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent target is a service-bearing commercial-equipment distributor, not a product-only catalog wholesaler. AI can improve a large information and coordination layer, while the physical installed base preserves technician and project accountability; the main constraint is that many firms in the code will not meet the service lens.
narrative.ai_impact: High-value workflows include product and parts identification, quote and submittal drafting, order entry, project scheduling, service intake, dispatch support, technician knowledge search, warranty documentation, purchasing, invoice matching, and customer updates. Installation, diagnosis in ambiguous field conditions, repair execution, and safety sign-off remain human work.
narrative.value_capture: Retention should be strongest in fixed-price annual maintenance, bundled projects, urgent repair, and internal overhead. Transparent equipment bids and catalog sales will pass more savings to customers because online, manufacturer-direct, and large-distributor alternatives make comparison easy.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen firm count covers an equipment-wholesale code with varied niches and business models. Older-owner evidence supports a succession pipeline, but neither the eligible service share nor the five-year qualifying-transfer rate is directly observed.
narrative.demand_durability: Commercial kitchens, stores, and institutions continue to need equipment uptime, parts, repair, compliance, and replacement. Equipment purchases are cyclical and currently soft in one major foodservice proxy, while service demand tied to the installed base should be steadier.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are product-only firms inside the code, thin or cyclical equipment margins, working-capital exposure, tariffs, manufacturer channel control, technician scarcity, and fragmented legacy data. AI may improve throughput without allowing headcount removal if branches remain subscale or service quality is brittle.
narrative.principal_driver: Physical installed equipment generates repeat service and parts workflows whose information and coordination layer is amenable to AI.
narrative.principal_weakness: Eligibility is uncertain because 423440 is fundamentally a heterogeneous merchant-wholesale category rather than a recurring-service category.

sources.title: Data tables for OEWS industry charts, May 2024

sources.title: GPTs are GPTs: An early look at the labor market impact potential of large language models
sources.supports: General U.S. task-exposure benchmark: about 80% of workers could have at least 10% of tasks affected and about 19% could have at least 50% affected.

sources.title: The Anthropic Economic Index
sources.supports: Observed Claude task use across occupations, including 57% augmentation and 43% automation, with stated representativeness limitations.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: 2019 Annual Business Survey graphic reporting that 51% of responding employer-business owners were 55 or older.

sources.title: The Metamorphosis of Women Business Owners: A Focus on Age
sources.supports: Census evidence that older employer owners are prominent in wholesale trade, used only as an owner-age proxy for succession.

sources.title: Global Industrial Company 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Distributor channel competition, customer buying criteria, service model, and workforce composition: 36% customer-facing, 26% distribution and fulfillment, and 37% administrative.

sources.title: Illinois Tool Works 2025 Annual Report
sources.supports: Commercial food-equipment products and integrated service model, including installation, annual service contracts, maintenance, repair, parts, and customer acceptance.

sources.supports: Commercial foodservice equipment demand proxy: 2025 sales decreased 1.7% excluding acquisition and currency effects, with weaker chain traffic and replacement demand cited.
